nativeRange.habitat: Neon Tetras are native to forest streams and minor tributaries with soft, acidic blackwater, where the substrate is typically sandy and covered with leaf litter, driftwood, and dense riparian vegetation.

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of foods including high-quality micro pellets, flake food, and frozen/live small invertebrates such as daphnia, bloodworm, and brine shrimp nauplii. Variety is recommended to maintain condition and colour.

avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Brandy Dopkins, 2521x1891. Sharp shoal of ~9 neon tetras in a planted aquarium; multiple individuals best represent this schooling species and the short mid-to-tail red stripe confirms the ID (vs. Cardinal Tetra).
Photo by Wojciech J. Płuciennik, 2802x1868. Crisp single specimen with vivid electric-blue and bright-red stripe in a lush green planted tank; classic hobby look, different angle for variety.
Photo by SOK, 4214x2194. High-resolution, tack-sharp macro side profile showing the iridescent blue stripe and red flank in fine detail against a soft gravel background.

summary: The Neon Tetra (Paracheirodon innesi) is one of the most popular freshwater fish in the hobby, known for its vivid iridescent blue-and-red stripe. Generally peaceful and tolerant of a range of water conditions, it does best in groups of eight or more in a planted tank.
careNotes:
The Neon Tetra is among the most widely kept freshwater aquarium fish in the world and is generally considered one of the more forgiving tetras for beginners. Neons tend to do best in soft, slightly acidic water that mimics their blackwater origin, though captive-bred strains have adapted over generations and commonly tolerate neutral to mildly alkaline tap water.

Neon Tetra Disease (NTD) is a well-documented threat: caused by the microsporidian parasite Pleistophora hyphessobryconis (sometimes cited as P. hyalinum in older literature), it presents as restless swimming, fading or loss of the characteristic blue stripe, white body patches, and eventual spinal curvature. There is no reliable cure once advanced; affected fish are typically euthanised. Commercial farms have historically been a vector, so rigorous quarantine of all new fish additions is strongly recommended.

A superficially similar condition, 'False NTD', is caused by the bacterium Flavobacterium columnare and can look nearly identical in early stages, but responds to antibacterial treatment.

Neons are also susceptible to ich (white spot) during cooler or fluctuating temperatures. Years of intensive commercial breeding have led to reduced genetic vigour in some ornamental strains; inspecting fish carefully for deformities and dull colouration at the point of purchase is advised.

Several selectively bred ornamental forms are available in the trade, among them albino, long-finned, short-bodied ('balloon'), diamond, and leucistic 'golden' variants. The Neon Tetra is distinguished from the closely related Cardinal Tetra (Paracheirodon axelrodi) by its shorter red stripe, which runs only from the mid-body to the tail; the Cardinal's red stripe extends the full length of the body. Neons are generally regarded as hardier and more tolerant of a wider parameter range than Cardinals.
breedingNotes: Breeding Neon Tetras is considered moderately difficult to challenging and requires a dedicated, dimly lit species tank, as the eggs and newly hatched fry are highly sensitive to light and must be kept in near-darkness. Soft, acidic water — pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5, and a temperature around 24–26°C — has been reported to produce the best results, though some breeders raise the temperature slightly to 26–28°C to trigger spawning. Neon Tetras are egg scatterers, meaning the adults broadcast eggs loosely over the substrate or plants rather than guarding a nest; because adults will consume the eggs, they must be removed from the breeding tank promptly after spawning. Eggs typically hatch within 24–36 hours and the fry become free-swimming after a further 3–4 days. Initial food for the fry is infusoria (microscopic aquatic organisms), which can be graduated to microworm and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii as the fish grow. Conditioning both sexes on live or frozen foods in the weeks before breeding is generally advised. Females are typically fuller-bodied than males when gravid.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source; comprehensive taxonomy, water parameters, native range, breeding notes, and disease information.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2.5 cm SL), temperature range (20–26°C), pH (5.0–7.0), and native range. Minor disagreement on pH upper bound: FishBase gives 7.0 vs SeriouslyFish 7.5; averaged to 7.0. Family listed as Acestrorhamphidae on FishBase page but Characidae is the accepted placement per higher taxonomy consensus and SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (1–1.5 in ≈ 2.5–3.8 cm), temperature (21–27°C), pH (6.0–7.0), gH (2–10), lifespan (6–8 years), and tank size (10 gal = 38 L). Tank size biased toward larger 60 L per playbook guidance and key data brief for proper schooling group. Size, temperature, and pH consistent across sources within averaging range.

fish.breedingNotes: Neon Tetras are egg scatterers that require a dedicated, soft-acidic, dimly lit spawning tank. The eggs and fry are sensitive to light, and adults will consume eggs if left in the tank after spawning. See breedingNotes for full detail.
